1: The dating formula is unusually precise: baššānâ hahîʾ bĕrēʾšît mamlekût Ṣidqiyyāhû... bašššānâ hārĕbîʿît baḥōdeš haḥămîšî — "in that same year, at the beginning of Zedekiah's reign, in the fourth year, in the fifth month." The dating aligns with the wider context of Jeremiah 27 where Jeremiah wore the wooden yoke before the envoys of surrounding nations. Hananiah the son of Azzur (ben-ʿAzzûr) is identified as "the prophet from Gibeon" — a named opponent who functions as a case study in false prophecy. The MKT preserves all the chronological markers because they function as verifiable historical grounding.
2: šābart ʾet-ʿōl melek Bābel — "I have broken the yoke of the king of Babylon"; Hananiah's oracle uses the same first-person divine voice as Jeremiah's oracles, the same divine titles (YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl), and the same prophetic formula (kōh-ʾāmar YHWH). The counterfeit oracle is structurally identical to genuine oracles — the only difference is content. The MKT renders Hananiah's oracle straightforwardly to show how plausible it sounded: "I have broken the yoke of the king of Babylon."
3: bĕʿōd šĕnatayim yāmîm — "within two years"; Hananiah's timeline — two years — is specific and testable. He specifies the return of the temple vessels taken under Jehoiakim (605 BC). The specificity of the timeline makes this prophecy falsifiable in a way that vague promises are not; the same specificity that lends credibility will ultimately expose it as false. The MKT renders "within two years" literally to preserve the testability.
4: Hananiah extends his oracle to include Jeconiah (Jehoiachin) himself and all the exiles of Judah who went to Babylon with him. The phrase nĕʾûm-YHWH (declares the LORD) closes the oracle — the same authenticating formula used in genuine prophecy. The comprehensive scope (temple vessels + the king + all exiles) makes this the most optimistic possible oracle at the most politically fraught moment.
5: Jeremiah's response is remarkable for its lack of immediate counter-attack: he speaks "in the presence of the priests and all the people who were standing in the temple" — the full public setting. The confrontation is not private; both prophets' reputations are on the line before the community. The MKT preserves the audience specification because the public nature of the confrontation gives the subsequent events their narrative weight.
6: ʾāmēn kēn yaʿăśeh YHWH — "Amen! May the LORD do so!" Jeremiah's startling "Amen" — he genuinely wishes the oracle were true. "May the LORD fulfill the words you have prophesied (yāqem YHWH ʾet-dĕbārêkā ʾăšer nitbāʾtā)" — an authentic expression of longing for the exile to end and the vessels to return. The MKT renders this as a sincere blessing rather than irony; Jeremiah's subsequent "but" in Jeremiah 28:7 is what introduces the challenge, not sarcasm in Jeremiah 28:6.
7: ʾak šĕmaʿ-nāʾ haddābār hazzeh — "But hear now this word"; the adversative ʾak (but, however) marks the transition from Jeremiah's sincere blessing to his challenge. The word Jeremiah speaks is addressed both to Hananiah and to "all the people" (bĕʾoznê kol-hāʿām). The public audience is maintained — the counter-challenge must be heard by all who heard Hananiah's oracle.
8: hannĕbîʾîm ʾăšer hāyû lĕpānay ûlĕpānêkā mēʿôlām — "The prophets who preceded you and me from of old"; Jeremiah appeals to the prophetic tradition as evidence. The historical pattern: authentic prophets (nābāʾ ʿal-ʾărāṣôt rabbôt wĕʿal-mamlākôt gĕdōlôt, against many lands and great kingdoms) characteristically prophesied war (milhāmâ), disaster (rāʿâ), and plague (deber). The MKT preserves the threefold list — war, disaster, plague — as the standard pattern of classical prophecy, which is the opposite of Hananiah's message.
9: hanābîʾ ʾăšer yinnābēʾ lĕšālôm — "As for the prophet who prophesies peace"; the epistemological problem of peace-prophecy. For judgment prophecy: the tradition and moral coherence of YHWH's character can validate it in advance. For peace prophecy: bĕbôʾ dĕbar hannābîʾ yiwwādaʿ hannābîʾ — "only when his word comes true will the prophet be known as one truly sent by the LORD." The future-fulfillment criterion applies uniquely to positive oracles. The MKT's "only when his word comes true" preserves the deferred-validation principle that will be vindicated in Jeremiah 28:17.
10: wayyiqqaḥ ḥananyāhû hannābîʾ ʾet-hammôṭâ — "Then Hananiah took the yoke bar"; mōṭâ (yoke bar, yoke pole) — the symbol Jeremiah had been wearing publicly. Hananiah's dramatic breaking of the bar (wayĕšabbĕrēhû) is a prophetic counter-sign: the yoke-breaking is his enacted oracle. In prophetic tradition, symbolic actions were not merely illustrative but effective; by breaking the yoke, Hananiah claimed to be enacting YHWH's redemptive act. The MKT renders the action narrative directly: "broke it."
11: kāzōʾt ʾešbōr ʾet-ʿōl Nĕbûkadrĕʾṣar melek Bābel — "This is how I will break the yoke of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon"; Hananiah interprets his own sign-act before the whole people. The public proclamation in Hananiah's words parallels YHWH's own proclamation-form. "And the prophet Jeremiah went his way" (wayyēlek yirmĕyāhû lĕdarkô) — Jeremiah's departure is significant: he does not counter immediately but waits for a fresh divine word.
12: The structure of divine response: Jeremiah does not speak on his own authority but waits for the word of YHWH to come (wayĕhî dĕbar-YHWH ʾel-yirmĕyāhû ʾaḥărê šĕbōr ḥananyāhû hannābîʾ ʾet-hammôṭâ). The temporal note "after Hananiah had broken the yoke bar" makes clear that this is a responsive word — YHWH addresses what Hananiah has done. The MKT preserves the causal relationship: the divine word responds to the prophetic action.
13: môṭôt ʿēts šābartā ûʿāśîtā taḥtêhen môṭôt barzĕl — "You have broken yokes of wood, but you have made yokes of iron to replace them"; the terrible irony: Hananiah's act of breaking the wooden yoke (ʿōl ʿēts) has resulted in an iron yoke (ʿōl barzĕl) being imposed. The symbolic act intended to enact liberation has enacted a heavier bondage. The MKT renders the irony starkly: wood broken, iron made.
14: ʿōl barzĕl nātattî ʿal-ṣawwĕʾar kol-haggôyim hāʾēlleh — "I have placed a yoke of iron on the neck of all these nations so that they may serve Nebuchadnezzar"; YHWH's own interpretation of the iron yoke. The phrase "including the wild animals" (wĕgam ʾet-ḥayyat haśśādeh nātattî lô) — the dominion given to Nebuchadnezzar is comprehensive, including the animal kingdom. The MKT preserves "wild animals" as a specific addition that emphasizes the totality of Babylon's divinely-granted sovereignty.
15: šĕmaʿ-nāʾ ḥananyāhû lōʾ-šĕlaḥăkā YHWH — "Listen, Hananiah! The LORD has not sent you"; the direct confrontation Jeremiah had withheld in Jeremiah 28:6-9 now comes. The triple disavowal of Jeremiah 14:14 (lōʾ šĕlaḥtîm... wĕlōʾ ṣiwwîtîm... wĕlōʾ dibbartî) is here compressed into the single decisive verb: "not sent you." The charge: wĕʾattâ hibṭaḥtā ʾet-hāʿām hazzeh ʿal-šāqer — "you have made this people trust in a lie."
16: hinnĕnî mĕšallĕḥăkā mēʿal pĕnê hāʾădāmâ — "I am about to remove you from the face of the earth"; the verb šālaḥ (send) appears ironically: YHWH did not send (šālaḥ) Hananiah (Jeremiah 28:15), but now YHWH is sending (mĕšallĕḥăkā) him — from life. "This very year you will die (tāmût), because you have spoken rebellion against the LORD" (sārāh dibbartā ʾel-YHWH). The specific "this year" (bašššānâ hazzōʾt) will be tested by Jeremiah 28:17.
17: wayyāmat ḥananyāhû hannābîʾ bašššānâ hahîʾ baḥōdeš haššĕbîʿî — "And the prophet Hananiah died that same year, in the seventh month"; the narrative fulfillment completes the prophetic contest. The seventh month (two months after the fifth-month confrontation of Jeremiah 28:1) and the same year — precise temporal verification. The narrative mentions Hananiah's title "the prophet" even in his death — the ironic designation of the false prophet who died. The MKT's literal rendering preserves the narrative's deliberate precision.
